definitions.text: Indicating movement towards or location at a higher place or position. Away from the surface of the Earth or other planet; in opposite direction to the downward pull of gravity.
examples: I looked up and saw the airplane overhead.
Shoots grow up and roots grow down.

definitions.text: Away from the surface of the Earth or other planet; in opposite direction to the downward pull of gravity.
examples: I looked up and saw the airplane overhead.
Shoots grow up and roots grow down.

definitions.text: To or at a physically higher or more elevated position.
examples: All day we climbed up and up.
Lift the carpet up and look underneath.
The cat climbed up the tree.
They walk up the steps.

definitions.text: Toward the center, source, or main point of reference; toward the end at which something is attached.
examples: The information made its way up the chain of command to the general.
I felt something crawling up my arm.
We sailed up the East Coast of England from Ipswich to South Shields.
Turn the cloth over so that the patterned side is up.

definitions.text: On or at a physically higher level.
examples: The flood waters are up again across large areas of the country.

definitions.text: Headed or designated to go upward (as an escalator, stairway, elevator etc.) or toward (as a run-up).
examples: Where is the up escalator?

definitions.text: The direction opposed to the pull of gravity.
examples: Up is a good way to go.

definitions.text: A positive thing, or a time or situation when things are going well.
examples: I hate almost everything about my job. The only up is that it's so close to home.
There are many ups to caravanning, but also many downs.
We upped anchor and sailed away.

definitions.text: To increase the level or amount of.
examples: If we up the volume, we may be able to hear what he says.
As usual, they've upped the prices for Valentine's Day.
It wasn’t long before they upped him to Vice President.
